1: Psalm 101 is one of the most distinctly royal psalms — a first-person declaration of governance principles by the king. Its Sitz im Leben may be the king's installation ceremony, a periodic covenant renewal, or a regular royal commitment. "Of David" (superscription) — the Davidic attribution makes the king the speaker throughout. "I will sing (ʾāšîrāh) of steadfast love (ḥesed) and justice (ûmišpāṭ); to you, LORD, I will make music (ʾăzammĕrāh)" — the king declares his governing values before declaring them as behavioral commitments. Ḥesed and mišpāṭ are the paired covenant attributes (Pss 33:5; 36:5-6; 89:14; 97:2; Isa 16:5) that define the ideal governance. The king who sings of these before YHWH places his governance within the divine governance, not as an independent program.
2: "I will conduct myself (ʾaśkîlāh) with wisdom and integrity (bĕdereḵ tāmîm). O when will you come to me (māṯay tābôʾ ʾēlāy)? I will walk (ʾeṯhallēk) with a blameless heart (bĕtōm-lĕbāb) within my own house (bĕqereb bêṯî)" — the personal character commitments before moving to governance. Śākal (to be wise, to act prudently, to prosper through wisdom) — the wisdom-governance of the Solomonic ideal (1 Kgs 3:12; 10:24). "O when will you come to me?" — a parenthetical yearning for divine presence; the king cannot govern wisely without divine accompaniment. This honest petition interrupts the declarations of commitment, acknowledging that the commitment requires ongoing divine support. "Blameless heart (tōm-lēb)" — integrity as inner orientation, not just external performance; the same term as Job's integrity.
3: "I will not place (lōʾ-ʾāšîṯ) anything vile (dĕbar-bĕliyaʿal) before my eyes. I despise the conduct (ʿăśôṯ-sĕṭîm) of those who fall away; it will have no hold on me (lōʾ yidbaṭ-bî)" — the king's visual commitment: he will not place bĕliyaʿal (worthlessness, wickedness, what is without value/Belial) before his eyes. In the ANE royal court, the king was surrounded by petitioners, counselors, entertainers, and symbols of power — the temptation to fill the visual and psychological space with what is vile (pornographic, violent, or idolatrous) was real and documented in the royal courts of the ancient world. Sĕṭîm (those who turn aside, apostates) — the falling-away from covenant commitment that the king explicitly repudiates.
4: "I will have nothing to do with a crooked heart (lēbāb ʿiqqēš); I will have no part in evil" — the inner and outer commitments reinforce each other: the crooked heart leads to evil action; rejecting the crooked person is protecting oneself from the influence that corrupts the inner life. ʿIqqēš (crooked, perverse, distorted) — the opposite of the straight path of wisdom (Prov 2:15; 8:8; 11:20; 17:20; 19:1; 28:6). The covenant community requires mutual integrity — the king who keeps crooked people close to him will be corrupted by their crookedness, no matter how pure his own intention.
5: "Whoever slanders (mĕlāšĕnî) his neighbor in private (bĕsēṯer), him I will silence (ʾaṣmîṯennû). The proud-eyed (gĕbah-ʿênayim) and the arrogant-hearted (ûrĕḥab lēbāb) I will not tolerate" — slander in private (bĕsēṯer) is more dangerous than open accusation because it cannot be easily refuted. The king's court was the setting in which political slander and false accusations were deployed to advance careers and destroy rivals; the king commits to silence (ṣāmaṯ, to destroy, to cut off) those who use such weapons. "Proud-eyed" — the eye as the organ of arrogance; haughty eyes (ʿênayim rāmôṯ) are the first item in Prov 6:16-19's list of seven things YHWH hates.
6: "My eyes will be on (ʿênay bĕ) the faithful people of the land (neʾĕmĕnê-ʾāreṣ), that they may live with me; only those who walk blamelessly (bĕdereḵ tāmîm) will serve in my presence" — the positive counterpart to vv. 3-5's exclusions: the king actively seeks the faithful. Neʾĕmān (faithful, trustworthy, reliable) from ʾāman (to be firm, to be reliable, to believe). "That they may live with me" — royal patronage and residential proximity in the palace were marks of favor and trust (cf. 1 Sam 18:2; 2 Sam 9:7, 13 — Mephibosheth ate at David's table). "Walk blamelessly (dereḵ tāmîm)" — the same integrity-walk of v2; the king selects for servants whose character mirrors his own commitment.
7: "No one who practices deceit (ʿōśēh rĕmiyyāh) will live in my house; no one who tells lies (dōbēr šĕqārîm) will remain in my presence (lĕnegel ʿênāy)" — the court purity code is extended into the palace itself. Rĕmiyyāh (deceit, slackness, treachery) — the slack bow of Ps 78:57 and the deceitful tongue of Prov 12:17; 14:5. "In my presence (lĕnegel ʿênāy)" — literally "before my eyes"; the king's gaze is the test. This is both a policy statement about royal court governance and a typological template for YHWH's own court: in the divine court, no deceit, no lying (Rev 21:27; 22:15). The ideal Davidic governance points forward to the King who will govern a fully purified court.
8: "Each morning (labbĕqārîm) I will silence all the wicked in the land, cutting off (lĕhakrît) every evildoer from the city of the LORD (mêʿîr YHWH)" — the daily, regular administration of justice. "Each morning" — the judicial session in the ancient Israelite city was held in the morning at the city gate (Jer 21:12: "administer justice every morning"; 2 Sam 15:2; Ruth 4:1-2). Lĕhakrît (to cut off, to eliminate) — the covenant penalty for the most serious offenses (Exod 12:15, 19; Lev 7:20-21; 18:29; 20:17-18). "The city of the LORD (ʿîr YHWH)" — Jerusalem as the city that belongs to YHWH; the king's justice is administration of the divine king's city on behalf of the divine king. The king's ultimate accountability is to the LORD whose city he governs.
